Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd put in a lot of hours—like 36 a week sometimes—but it wasn't organized in any real way. I'd listen to lectures and podcasts while doing other things, like commuting or working my part-time job. I found that auditory stuff stuck better for me, so I'd replay recordings multiple times rather than sitting down with a textbook.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline loomed, then I'd cram everything in at once. With 11 courses to juggle and limited resources, I couldn't afford to be super structured. I just did what I needed to survive each week, which meant my schedule shifted constantly based on what was due.
Q3: Usually it was a deadline or a notification that dragged me onto the LMS. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was something I'd missed or if grades had been posted. I wasn't someone who went on there just to browse—I needed a specific reason, like an assignment submission or finding a lecture I needed to download.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, but it was always task-driven rather than habitual. The closest it got to routine was when I had discussion posts due and I'd force myself to contribute something.
Q5: The recorded lectures were gold for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could download them and listen on repeat while doing other things. The discussion forums were also helpful because reading other people's perspectives sometimes clicked things into place for me better than the actual course materials did.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and PDFs. I know that sounds bad, but I just don't absorb written material well. The videos were also hit or miss—I'd skim them or watch at double speed. If something wasn't directly tied to an assessment, I'd push it to the bottom of my priority list and often never get to it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be assessed on, or if it actually helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. I didn't have the luxury of exploring for exploration's sake. Everything had to have a purpose, otherwise I'd feel anxious about wasting time I didn't have.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I'd see them and feel guilty for not doing them, which just added to my anxiety. Required ones at least had clear expectations—I knew what I had to do and when. Optional stuff made me feel like I was always falling behind, even when I was technically keeping up.
Q9: All the time. I'd download audio files and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while working. I also used external YouTube videos and podcasts that explained concepts better than some of the course materials. My actual studying happened mostly offline or through resources that wouldn't show up anywhere.
Q10: There were so many times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three times in one day while walking around, or I'd discuss concepts with classmates in person. My best studying happened when I was moving or doing something else—sitting at a computer staring at the LMS wasn't how I learned.
Q11: Constantly. I'd access something once just to download it, then never go back to that page again. The LMS would show one brief login, but I'd spend hours with that material offline. It was more practical for me given my limited internet access at home sometimes, and I could study without being tethered to my laptop.
Q12: My anxiety would spike randomly, and when it did, I'd completely shut down. Even though my stress was generally low, the anxiety was intense and unpredictable. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming. I'd avoid everything until the feeling passed, then scramble to catch up afterward.
Q13: Definitely. With low resources and multiple courses, I was always juggling. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by competing deadlines, the LMS became just another thing demanding my attention. I'd do the bare minimum online and focus my real energy on studying in ways that felt more efficient for me.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials completely zoned out. I'd have the LMS open but my brain would be elsewhere, worrying about money or other responsibilities. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't absorbing anything. It was just going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when my anxiety was high. I knew assignments were important, but starting them felt paralyzing sometimes. I'd tell myself I'd do it later, then later would become the night before it was due. The mindfulness exercises helped a bit, but the avoidance pattern was hard to break.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd barely touch the LMS until something was due, then I'd suddenly become very active. It wasn't healthy, but it worked for my grades somehow. The pressure of a deadline cut through my anxiety in a weird way—it forced me to just act instead of overthinking.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, though not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me avoid that course for a while. I'd eventually come back and use the feedback constructively, but my initial reaction was often to shut down.
Q18: The structure was a mixed bag. Having clear deadlines helped me function, but having so many courses meant I was constantly switching contexts. The discussion requirements actually got me interacting with peers more than I naturally would have, which was good. But the overall pace sometimes felt relentless.
Q19: The flexibility to access materials anytime was crucial for me since my schedule was unpredictable. But the sheer volume of content across 11 courses made it hard to engage deeply with anything. Shorter, more focused modules would have been easier to manage. The audio options were a lifesaver though.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things constantly without actually producing work. Or someone who's trying to understand the material but struggling to translate that into assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels or procrastinating by 'being busy.'
Q21: That's literally me. My attendance was terrible and I didn't spend much time on the LMS, but I scored really well on exams and assignments. I studied in ways the system couldn't see—listening to recordings, discussing with peers, using external resources. The LMS activity was just the tip of the iceberg.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and how long pages were open, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the mental processing that happens when you're not at a computer. They capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. For me, engagement happens mostly in my head and through conversations—it's not something that shows up in clicks and logins.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually struggled with anxiety while still managing to perform well. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to lectures while working my job, or the peer study sessions that helped things click. My real engagement was invisible to the system, but it was definitely there.
